meta.name: RCADS-25 Revised Child Anxiety and Depression Scale
meta.fullName: Revised Child Anxiety and Depression Scale - 25 Item
meta.description: The RCADS-25 is a 25-item short form assessing anxiety and depression symptoms in children and adolescents.
meta.author: Ebesutani C, et al. (2012)
meta.reference: Ebesutani C, et al. The Revised Child Anxiety and Depression Scale-Short Version: Scale reduction via exploratory bifactor modeling of the broad anxiety factor. Psychol Assess. 2012;24(4):833-845.
meta.instruction: Please indicate how often each item is true for you based on how you have been feeling recently.

questions.text: I worry about things.
When I have a problem, my heart beats really fast.
When I have a problem, I feel scared.
I worry that bad things will happen to me.
When I have a problem, I feel shaky.
I suddenly feel really scared for no reason at all.
I worry that I will suddenly get a scared feeling when there is nothing to be afraid of.
I am afraid of being in crowded places.
I worry about looking foolish in front of people.
I worry that I will do something to make a fool of myself.
I have to keep checking that I have done things right.
I am scared to go to school.
I worry what other people think of me.
I am afraid of talking in front of the class.
I am afraid of being alone.
Nothing is much fun anymore.
I feel sad or empty.
I am not interested in anything.
I have problems with my schoolwork.
I cannot do anything well.
I feel worthless.
I feel tired a lot.
I feel restless.
I have problems with my sleep.
I have no energy for things.

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your anxiety and depression symptoms are within the normal range. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining good mental health
Stay socially engaged and active
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life

ranges.label: Elevated Symptoms
ranges.description: Your anxiety and/or depression symptoms are elevated and deserve attention. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Talk to a trusted adult about how you are feeling
Maintain regular exercise and healthy sleep habits
Consider talking to a school counselor
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and avoid staying up late

ranges.label: Clinically Significant
ranges.description: Your symptoms reach clinically significant levels. Professional evaluation is recommended. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consult a mental health professional as soon as possible
Do not carry the burden alone; communicate with family or teachers
A professional assessment can help develop an appropriate intervention plan
Consider consulting a mental health counselor for professional guidance
Try self-help exercises based on cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)

anxiety.label: Normal
anxiety.description: Anxiety symptoms within normal range. This indicates that your anxiety levels are within the normal range, and your psychological state is relatively calm.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy lifestyle and coping strategies
Practice daily relaxation activities such as deep breathing or stretching
Keep up a regular exercise routine
Cultivate mindful awareness habits
Maintain strong social connections

anxiety.label: Elevated
anxiety.description: Elevated anxiety symptoms. Mild tension or worry is common in everyday life. Simple self-regulation techniques can often help manage these feelings.
suggestions: Learn and practice progressive muscle relaxation
Try 5-10 minutes of mindfulness meditation daily
Reduce caffeine and stimulant intake
Record situations that trigger anxiety and try to analyze them rationally
Exercise regularly, at least three times a week

anxiety.label: Clinical
anxiety.description: Clinically significant anxiety symptoms. At this level, you may find it difficult to relax, experience frequent worry, and notice effects on sleep or concentration.
suggestions: Consider consulting a counselor or psychiatrist
Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is highly effective for anxiety
Practice relaxation techniques daily and maintain regular exercise
Identify and reduce avoidance behaviors; gradually face anxiety-provoking situations
Maintain regular sleep and exercise habits

depression.label: Normal
depression.description: Depression symptoms within normal range. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life
Keep a consistent sleep schedule and exercise routine
Stay connected with friends and family

depression.label: Elevated
depression.description: Elevated depression symptoms. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and avoid staying up late
Practice mindfulness meditation or deep breathing exercises
Stay in touch with trusted friends or family members
Reduce time spent alone and engage in social activities

depression.label: Clinical
depression.description: Clinically significant depression symptoms. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health counselor for professional guidance
Try self-help exercises based on cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
Wake up at a fixed time each day to establish daily structure
Set small, achievable daily goals to gradually restore activity levels
Reduce alcohol intake and avoid unhealthy coping strategies
